chapters.title: When the road to your dream is paved with stumbles, it points to victory
chapters.text:
Almost everyone totes a childhood wish. We pictured ourselves as healers, space travelers, or maybe even Captain America. As we age, the world tilts and reality floods in. The writer and motivator Ryan Leak poses a sharp question, what would you attempt if you knew defeat was off the table?

Hoops dreams lit up Leak from a young age, and Kobe Bryant stood tall on his shelf of heroes. Even though his frame did not match the typical NBA body, he trained hard and clutched the dream. Life tossed him a chance to meet his hero face to face. Leak shared his scheme to film a documentary about chasing stumbles, and Bryant offered warm words of support. Then Ryan confessed he wanted a shot at the NBA. Bryant fired back with a simple, yes, go for it. Bryant holds the mark for the most missed attempts in league history, yet he stayed one of the greatest players ever because his triumphs outpaced his misses. Every successful figure becomes known not despite their misfires but because of them.

People stumble, and not everyone catches that a slip is a gift from fate. A stumble can reroute you to a path that fits better in the long run. If you never slipped, you would never finally land on top.

Leak set out to test the method of chasing stumbles with his own life. He wanted to chase an old wish and try out for the league. The moment he told his family, his friends, and Kobe Bryant that he would try out, there was nowhere left to hide. He fired off emails about his documentary idea to squads around the league. To his surprise, the Phoenix Suns wrote back. The head of public relations invited him to train with head coach Jeff Hornacek. Ryan Leak scored his first win in the chase. With every missed jumper, the author got exactly what he had come for. His nerve and his willingness to lose carried him further than a single-minded chase for victory ever could. He now works with NBA squads off the hardwood, helping them build their leadership.

Chasing Failure is not a book about trying to fail. It is a book about being willing to face losses on the road to a wish and about learning from the potholes and rocks along the way.

chapters.title: Your talents, chances, and goals shape your life work
chapters.text:
We often hear that we should never surrender our wishes, but sometimes it is hard to admit that the prize we are chasing is not worth the fight. When we are not scared of slipping, we can see what we really feel. Maybe dozens of no-thank-yous on The Voice mean that your strength is not in singing, and your real triumph is waiting in another field.

But some wishes should stay hobbies rather than lifelong pursuits. Leak tells the tale of a girl who had been tickling the keys since the age of three. She was sure that she would build her life around music, until one day she spotted a video of twelve-year-olds playing tunes she had spent years grinding through. Around that time, her interest in politics lit up at the University of Denver. She is now known as Condoleezza Rice, the sixty-sixth US Secretary of State.

Ryan Leak built what he calls the sweet spot matrix. It is the point where your fire, talents, openings, and goals overlap. Picture an activity that gets you out of bed in the morning and makes you lose track of time. If you can find a way to make that activity fun and pay your bills, pour yourself into it.

Savor every opening that teaches you something and helps you grow. If your podcast only has ten listeners, keep going, and there will soon be ten thousand. More chances come to people who genuinely love what they do.

Without openings and goals, wishes are out of reach. If you want to become an actor, go to local auditions or start a YouTube channel. Carve your own path to Hollywood. Mint your own chances, and pay attention to what your friends nudge you toward. They may see your strengths more clearly than you do.

If your work has no purpose, it will never feel complete. You can rake in cash and still feel hollow if the work is meaningless. Will reaching your goal help other people? The urge to help others drives much of our best work. You will discover that, when you slip up, your fire, goals, openings, and talents will quietly weed out the options that do not fit.

chapters.title: Success, like failure, comes with a price
chapters.text:
The word million is a kind of universal yardstick for what we want, whether we are talking about dollars, subscribers, or song downloads, the stuff of wishes. We count the rewards and ignore the criticism and the stumbles that show up on the way to that number. We do not want pain on the path to a bright future. We want to bathe in cash and love right now.

Talent can only get you so far, but hard work will keep you steady when you arrive at your destination, as Ryan Leak has said.

Leak invites you to answer five questions that help you see the real value of your wishes. First, how much time will you need? Leak suggests building a To-Don't-Do list, a place to capture tasks you can push to later. You have to understand how long it takes to win. Time management and planning have a huge impact on your triumph, so treat time like money. Save it and plan it.

Second, what disciplines will you need? Think about your sleep and your eating habits, and what keeps you healthy. Find activities that sharpen your mind. Third, will you need to invest money? Many people have brilliant ideas for apps or programs but no budget to bring them to life. A clear grasp of finance is essential to your idea. If you model or play sports, you will spend a lot on your health and your appearance, and that is an investment too. As you earn, remember to reinvest in your work to make it better.

Fourth, what should you learn? Study the victory stories of people who have made it in your niche. You have endless ways to learn, from books and podcasts to YouTube. Fifth, who do you need to know? Do not underestimate the power of a meeting. Value the people around you. Leak had a Bible teacher who once gave him the chance to speak at weekend services. In the audience sat the president of a mortgage company, who later invited Leak to speak at an event.

Every goal and every wish has a price.

chapters.title: Forgive yourself and release shame and fear of judgment
chapters.text:
In Shakespeare's play Cymbeline, there is a famous heroine named Imogen. Surprisingly, that is not her real name. She is Innogen, but the book was published with the wrong version. Ryan Leak had four editions of his own book, and he felt embarrassed to promote his work because of the slips. Shame and stumbling often travel together, but you cannot chase stumbles and stay in your comfort zone at the same time.

Any situation or any person can set off shame. Even if someone praises a friend's work, you may feel ashamed of your own. Leak suggests three ways to handle shame. First, do not expect to be perfect, because it is impossible. Social media builds an image of flawless lives. The Netflix film The Social Dilemma points out that Generation Z is the most exposed. They are afraid of getting a driver's license or going on a date. While the goal of social media is to make us more social, it often makes us feel guilty, ashamed, and on edge. Remind yourself that social media should not run your life. The internet is still a long way from reality.

Second, pay attention to the way you talk to yourself. Self-compassion is an art. Learn to forgive yourself, take care of your rest, and get enough sleep. Third, do not shame others. Build your empathy.

The two most powerful words when we are struggling are me too, as Brené Brown has said.

Ryan Leak proposed to his girlfriend, and they got married the same day in a surprise wedding. The moment was captured on video and went viral on YouTube. Millions of people loved it. At the same time, a hundred disliked it, and Ryan focused on the second group. But negativity can sharpen our flaws and help us become better. Sort the good from the bad and turn the bad into a benefit. Use criticism as fuel to sharpen your skills and grow as a professional.

Treat yourself the way you treat the people you love.

Atychiphobia is the irrational fear of failing to meet the standards and goals we set for ourselves or others. It can show up as anxiety about grades, losing a job, or hitting rock bottom in our self-esteem.

chapters.title: Risk is unavoidable if you want to change the world
chapters.text:
Procrastination looks like a blank document, a blinking cursor, an unfinished picture, a letter with no text and no address. It means you are stuck on an idea. When we cannot get started, dozens of what-ifs swarm in our heads. To build an action plan that actually works, answer three questions. What should I do this month to get closer to my goal? What needs to happen this week to reach the goal? What can I do today to move toward my wish?

A wish without a plan is just a hope, as Ryan Leak has said.

Daily goals are far more practical and far easier to reach than yearly goals. Aim for one win per day. Many of us do not want to launch a wish project until it is perfect. Stop trying to make your content perfect, and work to sharpen it instead. Another option is to split your week into themed tasks, and to use planners or any notebook you trust.

You have nothing to prove, but you have an endless supply of possibilities that you can keep improving.

Leak names three flavors of risk. The first is strategic risk. Our world keeps changing and growing more diverse. Leak tells his clients to update their technologies and strategies on a regular basis. Change can be tricky, but it is necessary. Former Disney president Bob Iger remembers going to Apple founder Steve Jobs for advice. Iger wanted Disney to buy Pixar. He showed Jobs a list of pros and cons, and the cons outnumbered the pros. Jobs said the benefits were much greater and that Iger should take the leap. That decision rewrote the history of Disney, and Pixar cartoons became one of the company's signatures.

The second flavor of risk is financial, and it can feel the most intimidating. Leak stresses the importance of having a budget to research and sharpen your idea, even when the investment is dicey. Sometimes financial risk means paying a coach to mentor your business.

Finally, take risks when you work with people, the third flavor of risk. If you are doing well, look around. There may be people waiting for you to reach out to them.

chapters.title: Bring in anyone brave enough to chase failure with you
chapters.text:
Your odds of triumph rise when you work with other people. Give people a chance to work on a dream project with you and to chase stumbles with you. You do not have to do it all alone. When you invite people into your goals, you get five benefits.

You get the chance of accountability. It is easier when someone helps you keep an eye on your progress. One of Ryan's friends dreamed of starting a podcast but could never find the time. Ryan offered a deal. He gave his friend three months to launch it, and if the friend slipped, he would buy Ryan a new, expensive drone. Ryan never got the drone, because the friend started the podcast. Find someone to help you stick to your promises.

You get the chance of help and support. When you tell someone about your business, that person will think of you when they hear about something that might help you.

You get the chance to help others. When you build new connections, think about the benefits for them and the ways you can help the other person.

You get the chance to learn if you let others down. When you slip up, be ready to own it. If you keep learning, you will stop slipping faster. Sometimes you are too proud to admit your slips, but you should not wallow in them. Apologize and move on.

You get the chance to learn when others slip up around you. Sometimes it is hard to let people into your wishes, and it is not easy to control everything. Sometimes you want to forgive others for their slips and move on, but do not be afraid to talk to people about the slips they have made. With your help, they may stop making them.

Help others to win, even if you are stuck on the path to your own wish.

We are afraid of stumbling, but we can beat the fear by being prepared, staying open to the lessons of life, and taking the risk of doing what feels impossible or out of reach.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
Many people treat Monday as the universal day to start changing their lives. Everyone dreams of becoming stronger and healthier, but only next year or next week. Do not wait for a better time. Become today what you want to be tomorrow.

Plans will never line up perfectly with what you might do today.

Start working on your plans for next year right now. There is always someone out there waiting for your results, a struggling student who will learn from your YouTube channel or a tired woman who will laugh at your stand-up routine.

Remember to ask questions during the conversation you have with yourself. Deep down, you already know the right answer, and a well-framed question will help draw it out of your subconscious. When you set a goal, plan how you will reach it and think about the obstacles that can trip you up. Remember that every wish has a price, and you will inevitably trade time or money along the way. In the process, you can find ways to waste less. The point is not to avoid stumbling. Let it greet you as the wisest teacher in your life. Take risks when the situation or trusted people tell you the risk will pay off. Your nerve will take you further than your caution.

Once you have sketched your wish, you are ready to chase stumbles. In the process, you will find endless possibilities and new friends. Ask yourself the right questions and remember to check that your wish blends openings, talents, and goals. Look stumbling in the eye, thank it for the lessons, and boldly follow your destiny.

Let failure remind you that, every day, you are getting closer to achieving what you once thought was impossible, as Ryan Leak has said.

Try this. Ryan Leak is an Enneagram trainer. The Enneagram is a system that sheds light on the hidden and unconscious causes of human behavior. It identifies nine personality types, which are the perfectionist, the helper, the motivator, the artist, the thinker, the loyalist, the enthusiast, the leader, and the peacemaker. Ask colleagues to take a personality test, which is easy to find online. Talk about the results and try to sort responsibilities by personality type. Think about the weaknesses of each type and how you can turn them into strengths. For example, the helper can be an excellent community manager, and the perfectionist will always find ways to polish the product.
